coredata.dc:title: Nanocellulose/carbon dots hydrogel as superior intensifier of ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite with adsorption and photocatalysis synergy for Cr(VI) removal

coredata.dc:description: A novel nanocellulose/carbon dots hydrogel (NCH) was fabricated using cellulose nanofibrils (CN), carbon dots (CD) and zinc oxide (ZnO)/silver bromide (AgBr) nanocomposite, where CD enhanced amino group-induced adsorption of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) and promoted the photocatalytic properties of ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite via the transfer of photogenerated electrons, resulting in enhanced efficiency in the removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution. The structure, morphology, and physicochemical properties of the prepared NCH were characterized, with the results of adsorption and photocatalysis experiments showing the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of the NCH to be 315 mg/g, 219 times that of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite; the apparent removal rate constant of the NCH was 0.0319 min−1, 11.7 times that of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite. Furthermore, the removal performance of NCH was attributed to CD-enhanced synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects, supported by characterization and experimental results. This work provides insight into the design and fabrication of a novel adsorptive photocatalyst with CD-enhanced synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects for removing Cr(VI) from aqueous solution.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nanocellulose/carbon dots hydrogel (NCH) was fabricated using cellulose nanofibrils (CN), carbon dots (CD) and zinc oxide (ZnO)/silver bromide (AgBr) nanocomposite, where CD enhanced amino group-induced adsorption of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) and promoted the photocatalytic properties of ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite via the transfer of photogenerated electrons, resulting in enhanced efficiency in the removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution. The structure, morphology, and physicochemical properties of the prepared NCH were characterized, with the results of adsorption and photocatalysis experiments showing the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of the NCH to be 315 mg/g, 219 times that of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite; the apparent removal rate constant of the NCH was 0.0319 min−1, 11.7 times that of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite. Furthermore, the removal performance of NCH was attributed to CD-enhanced synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects, supported by characterization and experimental results. This work provides insight into the design and fabrication of a novel adsorptive photocatalyst with CD-enhanced synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects for removing Cr(VI) from aqueous solution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is an extremely toxic heavy metal that is discharged from industrial settings, such as mining, electroplating, polishing, and battery manufacturing processes. This discharge of Cr-containing wastewater not only greatly damages the environment, but is also harmful to human health. A series of methods for the removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution have been proposed, such as chemical oxidation, piezocatalysis, photocatalysis, and adsorption [1]. Of these, adsorption is the widely preferred method due to its advantages of high efficiency, low cost, and convenient operation. Photocatalysis is also a viable method for the reduction of highly toxic Cr(VI) to less harmful Cr(III). The design of novel multifunctional materials that can perform both adsorption and photocatalysis is expected to significantly improve the removal efficiency of Cr(VI).

Hydrogels are considered to be effective adsorption materials for the removal of Cr(VI) ions from water. Pei et al. prepared a dual-network structured cellulose-based hydrogel and demonstrated its effective uptake of a low concentration of Cr(VI) [2]. Song et al. fabricated a carboxymethyl cellulose/polyethylenimine hydrogel, and showed that its Cr(VI) sorption capacity was 6.01 mmol/g [3]. Wang et al. synthesized a cellulose hydrogel, and demonstrated that the three-dimensional (3D) porous structure of this hydrogel acted as mass-transfer channel for Cr(VI) ions [4]. Although nanocellulose-based hydrogels have shown strong adsorption activity toward Cr(VI), the Cr(VI) ions are only concentrated on the hydrogels, leading to adsorption saturation and the creation of secondary pollution. Therefore, developing new nanocellulose-based hydrogels with synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects for treating Cr(VI)-contaminated water is necessary.

In recent years, zinc oxide (ZnO) has been widely in photocatalysis due to its high activity, low cost, and environmental friendliness [5]. Moreover, in the application of ZnO, constructing a heterojunction and reducing the size of the photocatalyst are becoming increasingly conventional. In particular, a ZnO/silver bromide (AgBr) nanocomposite has been demonstrated to exhibit remarkable photocatalytic performance in the removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution. However, ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite readily forms aggregates, which affects its photocatalytic performance. Moreover, ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite is very difficult to separate from treated water, which limits its recycling [6]. Fortunately, the development of a suitable carrier serves as a useful strategy for addressing these problems [7]. Nanocellulose-based hydrogels have great potential application as suitable carrier materials for this purpose. Furthermore, the immobilization of a ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite into a nanocellulose-based hydrogel may improve the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) via synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects.

Carbon dots (CD) are a class of carbon nanomaterials [8]. Luo et al. synthesized a 3D porous fluorescent hydrogel using amino-modified CD and cellulose nanofibers, and confirmed that the CD provided abundant sites for binding Cr(VI) ions [9]. Moreover, CD exhibit excellent electron transfer and storage performance. Wu et al. synthesized different CD/NaBiO3 hybrid materials that exhibit enhanced photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and indicated that the enhanced reduction was caused by the loading amount of CD [10]. Hence, CD can be used to improve the properties of nanocellulose-based hydrogels.

Herein, a novel nanocellulose/CD hydrogel (NCH) was reported, composed of cellulose nanofibrils (CN), CD and a ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite. In the NCH, the CN offered the “skeleton” of the hydrogel, from which a 3D porous structure was constructed; the CD enhanced the amino group-induced adsorption of Cr(VI) and accelerated the transport of photogenerated electrons; and the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite acted as a photocatalyst for generating photogenerated electrons; with the material showing superior removal efficiency of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution via synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects. Moreover, the morphology, structure, and photoelectric characteristics of the NCH were characterized, in addition to its adsorption and photocatalysis performance. On this basis, a possible mechanism was proposed to explain the effective removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution by the NCH.

Bleached wood pulp (approximately 5 g) was suspended in deionized (DI) water (100 mL) containing 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO, 0.04 g) and sodium bromide (NaBr, 0.20 g). Then, a solution of sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl, 30 g, 10 wt%) was added to the prepared solution dropwise at room temperature. Afterwards, the pH of the mixture was maintained at 10 by the addition of a solution of sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 0.1 M), with an oxidation time of 5 h. The mixture was then washed with DI water until neutral and homogenized using a M110P microfluidizer. After five cycles of high-pressure homogenization, the mixture was placed in regenerated cellulose dialysis tubes and dialyzed against DI water for 5 days. Finally, a CN suspension was obtained and stored at 4 °C.

Ethylenediamine (EDA, 536 μL) and citric acid (CA, 0.42 g) were added to DI water (10 mL). Next, the mixture was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (30 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After completion of the reaction, the reactor was cooled to 25 °C. The brown–black product was then dialyzed for 5 days. Finally, the CD were obtained and stored at 4 °C. Both the CN and CD were obtained as nanoscale materials, which were kept at 4 °C for long-term storage.

The CD were conjugated to the CN according to the following procedure. N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 0.19 g) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 0.23 g) were added to DI water (10 mL). This mixture was then added to a suspension of CN (25 g, 0.5 wt%), followed by dropping CD solution (2.5 g, 0.5 wt%) under vigorous stirring over a 12 h period. Afterwards, the mixture was dialyzed for 4 days. Finally, the concentration of the target product was adjusted to 0.3 wt% by the addition of a specific volume of DI water, and the product obtained was referred to as CN–CD.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1.8 g) and sodium citrate (4.2 g) were added to DI water (100 mL). Next, NaOH solution (20 mL, 2.0 mol/L) was added dropwise to the prepared solution under magnetic stirring over 4 h, after which a white precipitate was obtained. After centrifugation, washing and drying, ZnO nanoparticles were prepared. These ZnO nanoparticles (2.0 g) were added to DI water (100 mL) with vigorous stirring over a period of 1 h. Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB, 2.4 g) was then added to this solution, followed by the dropwise addition of silver nitrate (AgNO3, 0.42 g) in ammonia (25 wt% solution, 2 mL) with vigorous stirring at room temperature for 12 h. After centrifugation, washing and drying at 60 °C for 12 h, the product was obtained as a yellow powder. Finally, the powder was calcined in air at 500 °C for 3 h and confirmed as the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite.

First, acrylic acid (AA, 2.0 g) was dissolved in a solution of NaOH (5 mol/L, 4.72 mL) by stirring for 30 min in an ice water bath. Afterwards, CN–CD (2.0 g), N′,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 0.065 g) as a crosslinker, and a specific amount of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite (0, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, and 3.5 g) were mixed together for 2 h at room temperature. Next, the resultant solution mixture was purged with nitrogen gas for 30 min, and potassium persulfate (PPS, 0.03 g) was added. The mixture was then heated at 60 °C for 1 h. Finally, the product was immersed in DI water to remove any excess reagents and dried in freeze dryer for 3 days. The chemical structure of the obtained product was illustrated in Fig. 1 . According to the different masses of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite (0, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, and 3.5 g), the obtained products were named NCH-0, NCH-1, NCH-2, NCH-3 and NCH-4, respectively. The optimizations for the synthesis of NCHs were provided in Supporting Material. In addition, control nanocellulose hydrogels (NH) were synthesized using CN (2.0 g) instead of CN–CD (2.0 g). Masses of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite of 0 and 2.5 g were chosen, and the corresponding hydrogels were labeled as NH-0 and NH-2, respectively.

Adsorption experiments on the prepared samples were conducted in Cr(VI) solution. The effects of three factors including pH (1–6), initial Cr(VI) concentration (10–50 mg/L), and contact time (20–100 min) on Cr(VI) adsorption were studied. First, the prepared samples (0.11 g dry mass of the sample soaked in DI water for 4 h before use) were immersed in Cr(VI) solution (200 mL) in 250 mL conical flasks. The pH of the Cr(VI) solution was adjusted by adding appropriate amounts of NaOH and HCl. The conical flasks were fixed on a thermostatic shaker at a preset temperature and contact time. After that, aliquots of the solutions (10 mL) were collected and filtered using syringe filters, and their concentrations were determined using a UV–visible spectrophotometer via 1,5-diphenylcarbazide method at 540 nm. Measurements were conducted in triplicate to allow for the statistical evaluation of the results.

To investigate the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized samples, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution was determined using a 500 W high-pressure mercury lamp as the UV light source [11]. Before UV irradiation, the synthesized sample (0.11 g of dry mass soaked in DI water for 4 h before use) was added to the 200-mL Cr(VI) solution, and stirred at room temperature in the dark for 100 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. During the photocatalysis process, aliquots (5 mL) of the Cr(VI) solution were extracted and filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane every 20 min for subsequent analysis. The entire adsorption and photocatalytic process lasted 220 min.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-IT300L, Japan) was used to observe the morphology and microstructure of the prepared sample. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis of the prepared sample was performed using a spectrometer (Thermo Fisher IN 10™, USA), and the FTIR spectra with attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode were collected in the region of 4000–650 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/MAX2500, UK) data of the prepared sample were collected with Cu Kα irradiation. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Shimadzu UV-2450, Japan) of the prepared sample were collected in the region of 400–800 cm−1. Elemental analysis was performed by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Vg Scientific Sigma Probe, UK). Specific surface area was measured by a surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Tri-Star II, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were characterized by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000, Japan). Supporting material presents the detailed electrochemical measurement experiments.

NCH as a superior intensifier of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite was inspired by a computer chip comprising a collection device, a main processor, and an assistant processor, wherein the two processors processed the data, the collection device collected the data, and the entire computer chip exhibited high computational ability. Here, the NH, ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, and CD played the roles of collection device, main processor, and assistant processor, respectively. After the construction of a NCH from these components, the resulting material exhibited highly efficient removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution via synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects. A schematic illustration of the preparation route is shown in Fig. S1, featuring the preparation of CN, the synthesis of CD, the covalent bonding of CN and CD, and the free-radical polymerization process. First, the CN were synthesized via oxidation by TEMPO and high-pressure homogenization. Second, the CD were prepared via a hydrothermal method. Third, covalent bonds were formed between the CN and CD with the help of EDC/NHS, with the product referred to as CN–CD. Fourth, the CN–CD, AA, MBA, and ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite were thoroughly mixed under vigorous stirring. Finally, upon the addition of the PPS, free-radical polymerization occurred, which resulted in the formation of NCH.

The morphologies and structures of the CN, CD, CN–CD, ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, and NCH-2 were investigated by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2a, the CN had a smooth and fiber-like morphology. Moreover, the CD were spherical and monodisperse nanoparticles, exhibiting an average particle diameter of 3.0 nm (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the CN–CD presented a coarse surface (Fig. 2b), suggesting that the CD were grafted on the CN [12]. Fig. 2c shows a typical SEM image of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, which is consistent with the results previously published in the literature [13,14]. Furthermore, NCH-2 exhibited a 3D porous structure (Fig. 2d), the formation of which improved the swelling ability of the material (swelling ratio of 258 g/g for DI water at 25 °C). X-ray mapping analyses were conducted to determine the distribution of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite in NCH-2, as illustrated in Fig. S3, from which it was observed that ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite was homogeneously distributed on NCH-2.

The specific surface areas of the investigated materials were determined by conducting nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm measurements (Fig. S4). The specific surface area of the CN–CD was determined to be 7.02 m2/g, which was slightly higher than that of the CN (6.69 m2/g). This change was due to the modification of the CN with CD [15]. Among the measured samples, NCH-2 exhibited the highest specific surface area (22.0 m2/g) due to its 3D porous structure [16].

Fig. 2e shows XRD patterns of the prepared samples. For the CN and CN–CD, two identical XRD peaks were observed at 22.7° ((200) plane) and 14.9° ((1
1 ¯
0) plane), which confirmed that the crystal structure of the CN remained unchanged when combined with the CD in the CN–CD [17]. The XRD pattern of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite featured six peaks at 31.8°, 34.6°, 36.3°, 47.7°, 56.7°, and 68.1°, attributed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), and (200) crystal planes of ZnO, and four peaks at 31.1°, 44.4°, 55.1°, and 64.6°, corresponding to the (200), (220), (222), and (400) crystal planes of AgBr, respectively [18]. Additionally, it can be clearly observed that the XRD patterns of NCH-2 featured diffraction peaks of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, indicating that ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite was successfully incorporated into the NCH-2.

The FTIR spectra of the investigated materials are shown in Fig. 2f. For the CN and CN–CD, the band at 1054 cm−1 was assigned to a CO stretching vibration [19]. In addition, a peak related to a –CO–NH– stretching vibration could be observed at 1650 cm−1, further demonstrating the reaction between the amino group of the CD and carboxyl group of the CN [20]. In the spectrum of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, a peak corresponding to AgBr was located at 1509 cm−1 and the peak at 702 cm−1 could be attributed to ZnO bonding [21]. In the FTIR spectrum of NCH-2, the peak at 1054 cm−1 was obviously lower in intensity compared with that of the CN and CN–CD, further confirming that the CN–CD participated in the free-radical polymerization.

Fig. 2g shows the XPS survey spectrum of NCH-2, which exhibits signals that can be attributed to Zn, O, N, Ag, and Br. Fig. 2h shows the high-resolution Zn2p spectrum of NCH-2. The main peaks at 1022.2 and 1045.3 eV corresponded to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2, respectively, which were consistent with those observed for ZnO [22]. The O1s, N1s, Ag3d, and Br3d spectra of NCH-2 are shown in Fig. S5. Overall, the results confirmed that the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite was loaded into NCH-2.

Fig. S6 shows the effect of pH on Cr(VI) adsorption, indicating that the adsorption capacity of NCH-2 first increased upon an increase in pH from 1 to 2 and then decreased upon a further increase in pH. At pH = 1, chromic acid (H2CrO4) was found to be the dominant species in solution. Due to the weak electrostatic attraction between neutral chromic acid and NCH-2, the adsorption capacity of NCH-2 was low. In the pH range of 2–6, HCrO4 − and Cr2O7 2− were the dominant species, and an electrostatic force was present [23]. With an increase in pH, the degree of protonation of NCH-2 decreased, resulting in a decrease in its adsorption capacity [24]. In addition, the point of zero charge (pzc) of the adsorbent also plays a crucial role in Cr(VI) adsorption [25]. When the solution pH is less than the pzc, the adsorption of an anionic pollutant is more favorable [26]. In this case, the pzc of NCH-2 was found to be 2.3 (Fig. S7), therefore, the solution pH was lower than 2.3, NCH-2 exhibited good adsorption capacity for anionic Cr(VI). Hence, the optimal pH for Cr(VI) sorption by NCH-2 was determined to be 2.

The adsorption capacities of the hydrogel samples NH-0 (in the absence of CD and the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite), NH-2 (in the absence of CD) and NCH-2 at different initial Cr(VI) concentrations (C 0) are shown in Fig. 3a. As the initial Cr(VI) concentration increased from 10 to 50 mg/L, there was an increase in the adsorption capacities of NH-0, NH-2, and NCH-2. Notably, at the same initial concentration, the adsorption capacity of NCH-2 was greater than those of NH-2 and NH-0, confirming that the loading of CD and ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite enhanced the adsorption capacity of NCH-2 [9]. Two isotherm models, i.e., Langmuir and Freundlich, were used to explain the mechanism of the sorption between Cr(VI) and NCH-2 (Fig. 3b, c), with Table S1 listing the corresponding fitting parameters. Based on the coefficient of determination (R 2) values, the adsorption of Cr(VI) by NCH-2 was most suitably modeled using the Langmuir model, suggesting the monolayer adsorption [27]. Furthermore, according to the Langmuir model [28], the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of NCH-2 was 315 mg/g, approximately 219 and 2.60 times higher than that of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite (1.44 mg/g, as shown in Fig. S8) and NCH-0 (121 mg/g, as shown in Fig. S9), respectively. More importantly, this value is one of the highest adsorption capacities measured for Cr(VI) ions to date (Table 1 ). Thus, this high adsorption capacity demonstrated that NCH-2 exhibited good adsorption performance of Cr(VI) ions.

As shown in Fig. 3d, the adsorption kinetics of NH-0, NH-2, and NCH-2 toward Cr(VI) were determined at room temperature. Overall, the results showed that an increase in the contact time led to an increase in adsorption capacity. More importantly, at the same time, the adsorption capacity of NCH-2 was greater than that of NH-0 and NH-2, indicating that the addition of CD and ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite was beneficial for Cr(VI) sorption [9]. Two kinetic models, namely, pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order models, were applied to fit the adsorption process of NCH-2 (Fig. 3e, f), with the corresponding fitting parameters listed in Table S2. Based on the R 2 values, it was confirmed that the pseudo-second-order model was more suitable for the obtained data, demonstrating that the chemisorption process was the rate-controlling step in the sorption of Cr(VI) to the NCH-2 [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was evaluated via the photoreduction of Cr(VI) under UV light irradiation. Before irradiation, the reaction mixture was stirred for 100 min in the dark to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Fig. 4a shows the Cr(VI) removal efficiencies of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, NCH-0, NH-2, and NCH-2 as a function of time. After 120 min of UV light irradiation, the efficiencies of the removal of Cr(VI) over there samples were 31.3 %, 23.7 %, 86.4 %, and 96.2 %, respectively. This result indicated that NCH-2 exhibited the best photocatalytic reduction performance. Furthermore, the photocatalytic reaction rate could be well described by pseudo-first-order kinetics (Fig. 4b). It could be seen that NCH-2 possessed the highest apparent removal rate constant (k, 0.0319 min−1), around 11.7, 17.2, and 1.91 times higher than those of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite (0.00273 min−1), NCH-0 (0.00186 min−1), and NH-2 (0.0167 min−1), respectively. NCH-2 exhibited better Cr(VI) removal performance compared with the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite due to the construction of a 3D porous structure. The construction of the 3D porous structure in NCH-2 increased its specific surface area and provided additional active sites, thereby improving its removal efficiency. The NCH-2 exhibited higher Cr(VI) removal efficiency than NCH-0 due to the loading of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite. The loading of ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite improved the removal efficiency via the synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects. Furthermore, NCH-2 exhibited higher Cr(VI) removal efficiency than NH-2 due to the loading of CD. The loading of CD enhanced the amino group-induced adsorption of Cr(VI) and improved the photocatalytic properties of ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, thereby improving its Cr(VI) removal efficiency.

Fig. 4c shows the effects of pH on the removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution using NCH-2. Over a period of 120 min, 96.2 %, 57.5 %, 49.6 % and 36.8 % of Cr(VI) present in solution was reduced at pH values of 2, 3, 4, and 5, respectively, suggesting that acidic conditions were favorable for the removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution by NCH-2. The kinetics underpinning the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) at these four different pH levels were fitted using a pseudo-first-order equation, as shown in Fig. 4e. The k values of NCH-2 at pH 2, 3, 4, and 5 were 0.0319, 0.00620, 0.00510, and 0.00320 min−1, respectively. It was clear that the pH could play a significant role in Cr(VI) photocatalysis. As the pH increased, the protonation of NCH-2 was weakened, resulting in a decrease in adsorption capacity [24]. Meanwhile, the concentration of hydrogen ions in the solution decreased, resulting in unfavorable structural changes on going from Cr(VI) to Cr(III) [43]. From the results, an optimal pH of 2 was chosen for subsequent experiments.

Fig. 4e shows the effect of the amount of ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite loaded into NCH. With an increase in the amount of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, the removal efficiencies of NCH-x (x = 1, 2, 3, and 4) first increased and then decreased. The kinetic process of the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by the different NCH-x materials was fitted using a pseudo-first-order equation, with the results shown in Fig. 4f. It could be seen that NCH-2 exhibited the highest k (0.0319 min−1), which was around 5.06, 1.72, and 2.45 times higher than those of NCH-1 (0.00630 min−1), NCH-3 (0.0185 min−1), and NCH-4 (0.0130 min−1), respectively. The results thus confirmed that NCH-2 contained the optimal loading amount of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite [44].

As shown in Fig. 4g, the effect of the initial concentration on the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution was evaluated by varying the concentration from 10 mg/L to 40 mg/L. The lower the concentration of Cr(VI), the greater the reduction of Cr(VI). For initial Cr(VI) concentrations of 10 mg/L and 20 mg/L, almost 100 % of Cr(VI) was removed from aqueous solution within a period of 120 min. Moreover, the photocatalytic reaction rate of NCH-2 with different concentrations of Cr(VI) can be described using pseudo-first-order kinetics (Fig. 4h). The k values of NCH-2 at concentrations of 10, 20, 30, and 40 mg/L were 0.0594, 0.0319, 0.0102, and 0.00666 min−1, respectively. The competition of Cr(VI) for photocatalyst active sites was thus reinforced at a high initial concentration [45].

Reusability is an important factor in verifying the stability and recyclability of a material. Therefore, the NCH-2 sample was used for five consecutive cycles in the removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution, with the results shown in Fig. 5a. After each run, the NCH-2 sample could be easily removed from the reaction vessel without filtration or centrifugation, and immersed in NaOH solution (0.1 M) for 5 h. After 5 h of immersion, the NCH-2 sample was removed from the NaOH solution, washed with DI water five times, and reused in the next run. After five cycles, the removal activity of NCH-2 was still >88 % of its initial value. Furthermore, FTIR spectra and XRD data were used to confirm the structural stability of NCH-2 after being recycled five times (Fig. 5b, c). The chemical groups and crystal structures of NCH-2 after five recycling experiments were in agreement with those of pristine NCH-2, indicating that NCH-2 exhibited good stability over five consecutive cycles [46]. However, from a comprehensive and critical viewpoint, the use of NCH-2 as an ideal adsorbent still requires further improvement in terms of its stability and recyclability.

First, FTIR and XPS spectra were used to examine the mechanism of the adsorption of Cr(VI) on NCH-2. The FTIR spectrum of NCH-2 (Fig. 6a) before sorption shows a peak at 3323 cm−1, which can be attributed to NH and OH stretching vibrations [41]. However, after adsorption, a decrease in the characteristic intensity of the 3323 cm−1 peak was observed. Meanwhile, a new peak emerged at 902 cm−1, which could be attributed to a Cr species [47]. From the data, it was confirmed that the amino group took part in Cr(VI) sorption. Since the CD were rich in amino groups, the results indicated that the CD had a positive effect on Cr(VI) sorption.

Fig. 6b shows the XPS survey spectra of NCH-2 before and after adsorption. A new peak at 587 eV corresponding to the Cr species was observed after the sorption process, confirming that Cr(VI) adsorbed onto NCH-2 [48]. Fig. 6c shows the Cr2p spectrum of NCH-2, which is deconvoluted into four components at binding energies of 576.1, 579.1, 585.8, and 588.2 eV. The first two components were related to Cr2p3/2 orbitals, and the other two to Cr2p1/2 orbitals, respectively. More importantly, the two component peaks at 579.1 and 588.2 eV were assigned to Cr(VI), while the two component peaks at 576.1 and 585.8 eV were assigned to Cr(III). The appearance of these peaks corresponding to Cr(III) proved that the Cr(VI) ions were reduced to Cr(III) [40].

The high-resolution O1s spectrum of NCH-2 before adsorption (Fig. 6d) displayed three component peaks at 531.0 (ZnO), 531.6 (CO), and 533.0 eV (CO). After adsorption, these peaks shifted to 530.7, 531.5, and 532.8 eV, respectively, with these changes indicating the electrostatic attraction between –COOH and Cr(III) ions [49].

The high-resolution N1s spectrum of NCH-2 before the adsorption of Cr(VI) displayed three peaks at binding energies of 402.4 (–NH3 +), 399.9 (–NH2), and 399.3 eV (N) (Fig. 6e). After adsorption, these three peaks shifted to 402.6, 400.0 and 399.4 eV, respectively, and a clear increase of the peak at 402.6 eV was observed. These results confirmed the enhancement of the amino group for Cr(VI) adsorption [37].

The optical properties of the prepared samples were studied by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 7a, the absorbance of the as-synthesized NH-2 was much lower than that of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite due to the dilution of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite. Moreover, the absorbance of the NCH-2 was higher than that of NH-2, which was due to the loading of the CD. The band gaps of the studied materials were calculated, with the results shown in Fig. 7a, where it can be observed that the band gaps of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, NH-2, and NCH-2 are 3.05, 3.02, and 2.97 eV, respectively. Compared with the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite and NH-2, NCH-2 had a smaller band gap, suggesting that NCH-2 exhibited better light absorption performance [50].

Fig. 7b shows the valence band XPS spectra of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, NH-2, and NCH-2, where their valence bands are located at 2.61, 2.20, and 1.90 eV, respectively. Compared with the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite and NH-2, NCH-2 exhibited a more positive valence band. The conduction band can be calculated from the band gap and the valence band using the equation E C = E B − E g, where E C is the conduction band, E B is the valence band, and E g is the band gap. Therefore, the E C values of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, NH-2, and NCH-2 were calculated to be −0.44, −0.80, and −1.07 eV, respectively.

Fig. 7c shows the PL spectra of the prepared samples. Compared with the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite and NH-2, NCH-2 exhibited the lowest PL emission intensity, suggesting that it had the highest separation efficiency of electron–hole pair. Therefore, the construction of the 3D porous structure and the loading of CD could reduce the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic performance of NCH-2 [51].

The kinetics of charge carriers were studied using time-resolved PL spectroscopy (Fig. 7d). NCH-2 had a shorter fluorescent lifetime (0.69 ns) compared with NH-2 (1.45 ns), which was due to the nonradiative pathway of charge carriers through the connection between ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite and CD [52].

Fig. 7e shows the transient photocurrent responses of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite, NH-2, and NCH-2 under intermittent UV light irradiation. NCH-2 presented the highest photocurrent response, indicating its superior photoinduced charge carrier separation efficiency compared with the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite and NH-2 [53].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed to study interfacial charge transfer behavior. In the EIS Nyquist plots, the arc radius of NCH-2 was much smaller than that of the other samples, indicating its efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers [21]. Overall, the above analysis and discussion confirmed the enhanced photoelectric performance of NCH-2 compared with the other studied materials.

To elucidate the main active species of the photoreduction activity of Cr(VI), the role of radical scavengers was studied. Here, ethanol, isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ), and AgNO3 were used to quench the holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (OH), superoxide anions (O2 −) and electrons (e−), respectively [54]. Fig. 8a shows the effect of different scavengers on the removal efficiency of NCH-2. The addition of BQ and AgNO3 resulted in a decrease in photoreduction activity, indicating that O2 − and e− were the active reactive species. After introducing ethanol into the system, the removal efficiency increased, which may be due to the consumption of h+ inhibiting the recombination of h+ and e−, thus promoting the action of e− [55–57]. It was thus confirmed that e− was one of the active species. Moreover, the removal efficiency decreased in the presence of BQ and AgNO3. The k values were 0.0739, 0.0278, 0.0140, 0.00925, and 0.0319 min−1 with the addition of ethanol, IPA, BQ, AgNO3 and no scavenger, respectively (Fig. 8b). These values further implied that e− and O2 − were responsible for the reduction of Cr(VI). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spin-trapping using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) measurements were conducted to further confirm the function of O2 − radicals. The signal for DMPO-O2 − was clearly observed after an irradiation time of 20 min (Fig. 8c). For NCH-2, since the CD acted as an electron acceptor [58], the loading of CD was beneficial to the generation of e− and O2 −.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of CD-enhanced adsorption and photocatalysis synergy
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanism of the CD-enhanced synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis effects was proposed (Fig. 9 ). First, the 3D porous structure of NCH features large active sites, such as the amino group of the CD. Due to the strong electrostatic attraction between the protonated amino group of the NCH and Cr(VI) ion, the Cr(VI) ion is rapidly adsorbed on the surface of the NCH. Under UV light irradiation, the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite is excited to generate photogenerated electrons. The doping of CD facilitates the separation and transfer of these photogenerated electrons. More importantly, due to the construction of a 3D porous structure, the light harvesting ability of NCH is improved, resulting in the generation of more photoinduced electrons. The photogenerated electrons that accumulate in the CD are then captured by adsorbed Cr(VI) ions to form Cr(III). Moreover, some photogenerated electrons combine with O2 to generate O2 −, which then further reduce Cr(VI) ions to form Cr(III) [59]. Due to the electrostatic repulsion between the amino-modified CD and Cr(III) ions and the coordination bond between the NCH and Cr(III) ions, the Cr(III) ions leave the surface of CD, freeing up active sites for the next adsorption process. Therefore, the main reaction steps involved in the removal of Cr(VI) by the NCH can be expressed using the following equations.

In summary, a method was proposed for the synthesis of a novel NCH comprising CN, CD and a ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite for efficient removal of Cr(VI) from aqueous solution. The NCH was a superior intensifier of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite as it exhibited CD-enhanced synergetic adsorption–photocatalysis effects. The as-prepared NCH exhibited excellent performance in the removal of Cr(VI), with a theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of 315 mg/g and a removal rate constant of 0.0319 min−1, which were 219 and 11.7 times higher than those of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposite. Based on the characterization and experimental results, this excellent performance was attributed to the CD-enhanced synergistic adsorption–photocatalysis effects. Therefore, NCH was a promising adsorptive photocatalyst for use in the removal of Cr(VI) ions from aqueous solution.
